structural_units: AlO6 octahedra and BeO4 tetrahedra in orthorhombic framework
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Chrysoberyl is an orthorhombic oxide with Be in tetrahedral and Al in two distinct octahedral sites. Alexandrite occurs when Cr3+ substitutes for Al3+ in sufficient quantity to produce the alexandrite effect. The dual absorption bands in the visible spectrum create the transmission window responsible for color change.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: The alexandrite effect requires Cr3+ in an orthorhombic field (different from trigonal corundum). Two broad absorption bands in the blue-yellow region and a transmission window in both the red and green-blue create a near-balance that shifts depending on illuminant. Tungsten light emphasizes red transmission; daylight/fluorescent light emphasizes blue-green. Vanadium also contributes to color change in some specimens.

crystallography.twinning: Polysynthetic twinning extremely common — chrysoberyl and alexandrite twins create the characteristic 'trillings' (cyclic triple twins) pseudohexagonal rosettes.
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Prismatic tabular crystals with rhombic cross section. Characteristic cyclic trillings (three intergrown crystals at 120° forming pseudohexagonal rosettes) are diagnostic of chrysoberyl. Crystals often have characteristic V-shaped striations. Heart- or leaf-shaped twins common.

physical_properties.physical_notes: High hardness (8.5 — between topaz and corundum); third hardest natural gemstone. High SG for a non-oxide gem. Very durable for jewelry use. No perfect cleavage in gemologically critical direction makes it suitable for cutting.

color_profiles.name: Classic Alexandrite Color Change
colors: emerald-green to bluish-green in daylight; red to purplish-red in incandescent light
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ in BeAl2O4 orthorhombic field: dual transmission windows in red and blue-green of visible spectrum; daylight (blue-rich) emphasizes green transmission; incandescent (red-rich) emphasizes red transmission.
color_profiles.conditions: Strong color change requires high Cr content and balanced transmission. Ural Mountains (Russia) specimens show finest change. Modern sources (Brazil, Sri Lanka, Africa) variable.

color_profiles.name: Brazilian Alexandrite
colors: brownish-green to brownish-red; less vivid than Russian
color_profiles.cause: Same Cr3+ mechanism; slight Fe contamination shifts color toward brown.
color_profiles.conditions: Hematita and Malacacheta, Minas Gerais, Brazil.

color_profiles.name: Cat's Eye Alexandrite
color_profiles.cause: Color change from Cr3+ combined with chatoyancy from parallel tube inclusions or needle inclusions.

common_forms: faceted oval, cushion, or round gems
appearance.visual_notes: Alexandrite is unique — a single stone with two identities. Under northern daylight or fluorescent light it is distinctly green; under incandescent (candle, tungsten) light it turns vivid red-purple. The best Russian specimens shift from 'emerald' to 'ruby' — an impossible color change nicknamed 'the emerald by day, ruby by night' gem. No other natural gemstone produces such a dramatic and complete color change.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Moderate red fluorescence under UV (Cr3+ emission, same mechanism as ruby)
optical_and_special_effects.chatoyancy: Cat's eye alexandrite — strong chatoyancy from oriented needles; combines color change + cat's eye
optical_and_special_effects.asterism: Extremely rare four-rayed star alexandrite documented
optical_and_special_effects.pleochroism: Trichroic: red / orange / green (distinct in three crystallographic directions)
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Biaxial positive. RI approximately 1.746–1.755. Birefringence ~0.009. Strong trichroism means orientation critical for color in faceting. The alexandrite effect is observable spectroscopically — characteristic chromium absorption bands.

geologic_environments: Cr-bearing metamorphic rocks (emerald-like conditions but with Be)
beryllium-bearing granitic pegmatites intersecting Cr-rich schist or ultramafic rocks
notable_localities: Tokovaya River, Ural Mountains, Russia (original discovery 1834 — finest quality)
Ratnapura, Sri Lanka (cat's eye alexandrite; smaller production)
Zimbabwe (Sandawana — small stones, excellent color change)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Alexandrite requires an improbable geological coincidence: beryllium-rich fluids must encounter chromium-rich host rocks — two elements that rarely coexist in the same geological setting. The Ural Mountain deposits (Tokovaya River) were the sole significant source for much of the 19th and early 20th centuries. Brazil is now the primary commercial source.

birthstone_status: June birthstone (modern — alongside pearl and moonstone)
historical_uses: Russian imperial gem during Romanov dynasty
Tsar Alexander II's stone — named on his 16th birthday (April 17, 1834)
cultural_associations: Named for Tsar Alexander II of Russia — discovered on his birthday (April 17, 1834)
Colors of Imperial Russia: red and green (army uniform colors)
Considered a stone of good omen in Russia — wearing two gemstones in one
Modern rarity has elevated it to extreme collector status
symbolism: good luck and balanced opposing forces
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Alexandrite was officially discovered on April 17, 1834 — the 16th birthday of Crown Prince Alexander II of Russia, who would later become Tsar. The stone was named in his honor by mineralogist Nils Gustaf Nordenskiöld. Its green-and-red color change matched the military colors of Imperial Russia, making it doubly auspicious. Russian jewelers favored it highly until the Revolution. Until the 1950s, alexandrite was one of the rarest colored gemstones; Brazilian deposits have made it more available, though fine Russian stones remain extreme rarities.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Alexandrite is the great paradox of gemology — one stone, two completely different identities, perfectly opposed. Green by day, red by night. The Russian gem named for a Tsar it would outlast by centuries. No other gem changes so completely.

practical_notes.toxicity: Non-toxic. Beryllium in mineral form is not hazardous when handled normally.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Very hard and durable — suitable for all jewelry use. No concerning cleavage for wear. Safe for daily use.
practical_notes.care_notes: Clean with warm soapy water. Ultrasonic generally safe. Store separately from harder/softer gems.
treatments_or_synthetics: Flux-grown synthetic alexandrite (Chatham, Creative Crystals): shows curved growth lines and flux inclusions
Pulled synthetic (Czochralski) alexandrite: nearly inclusion-free; requires spectroscopic testing
Color-change glass simulants (vanadium glass): show color change but lower RI and SG
Color-change sapphire sold as alexandrite (different RI and color shift direction)
Color-change spinel: similar effect; different RI
common_confusions: Synthetic alexandrite (requires inclusion and spectroscopic analysis)
practical_notes.market_notes: Natural fine alexandrite is extremely rare and commands very high prices. Russian Ural specimens are the rarest and most valuable. Brazilian is more available but still expensive for good color change. Cat's eye alexandrite is exceptionally rare. Synthetic is widely available and openly sold as synthetic. The color-change percentage (degree of shift) is the primary value factor.

search_boost_terms: emerald by day ruby by night
prompt_fragments: alexandrite: green under daylight, turning deep red-purple under incandescent bulb
Ural Mountains alexandrite — Tsar's gem shifting colors like Imperial Russia's uniform
emerald by day, ruby by night — the chromium paradox of chrysoberyl

provenance.notes: Alexandrite is a variety of chrysoberyl (BeAl2O4). mindat_id 92 is the chrysoberyl/alexandrite entry.
